skill.name: google-agents-cli-deploy
skill.description: Guides deployment of Google ADK agents to Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE using agents-cli, Terraform, CI/CD, IAM, Secret Manager, and testing workflows.
skill.summary: Deploy and operate Google ADK agents on Google Cloud targets.
skill.author: google

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical command, network, and credential combination, but review found Markdown deployment guidance rather than executable malicious code. The external command and network detections are mostly documentation examples; the remaining risk is operational because users may run cloud deployment, IAM, Terraform, and secret-related commands.

medium_findings.title: Infrastructure-Changing Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run agents-cli, gcloud, kubectl, git, gh, and Terraform-related commands that can deploy services, alter IAM, change traffic, or modify cloud resources. This is intended for a deployment skill, but it requires explicit approval and careful project selection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show deployment, API enablement, rollback, and infrastructure commands. The context is legitimate documentation, so the risk is operational misuse rather than hidden code execution.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Secret Handling Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill covers API keys, database passwords, GitHub PATs, Secret Manager, and environment variable access. The examples use placeholders and recommend Secret Manager, but users could expose real credentials if they paste values into prompts, logs, or shell history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly discuss secret values, Secret Manager access, and a GitHub PAT flag. The guidance is normal for deployment, but credential mishandling remains a plausible user-facing risk.

medium_findings.title: Network-Exposed Deployment and Testing Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill discusses public Cloud Run ingress, deployed service URLs, authorization headers, IAP, PSC, and internal GKE access. These are expected deployment topics, but incorrect settings could expose agent endpoints or cause unauthorized access failures.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show real network configuration and testing instructions. The examples are not malicious, but deployment exposure depends on the user environment and chosen settings.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution Hits Are Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick and command-substitution detections come from Markdown code spans, shell examples, tables, and documentation text. No evidence found that the skill itself contains executable scripts or hidden code that automatically runs these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation and fenced examples, not runnable skill code. The risk remains only if an assistant or user chooses to execute the documented commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Values Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and IP detections are example service URLs, localhost testing addresses, and links to official documentation or sample repositories. No evidence found of data exfiltration endpoints or unauthorized background network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples and documentation references. The local addresses are used for explicit testing after deployment, not hidden communication.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Detections Are Context Mismatches
low_findings.description: Static high findings for weak cryptographic algorithms did not correspond to instructions to use MD5, SHA-1, or obsolete encryption. The flagged areas are deployment metadata, Terraform snippets, Cloud SQL notes, or authentication examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no weak cryptographic algorithm recommendation. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated deployment and auth terminology.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Execution of Cloud Deployment Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many command examples that can change Google Cloud infrastructure, deploy services, or update IAM when executed. This pattern is legitimate for the skill, but should be gated by user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and potentially state-changing. They are not automatically executed by the skill, which limits severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Names and Token Placeholders in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include API key, database password, authorization token, and GitHub PAT placeholders. They are placeholders, but they create a user education risk around pasting real secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential-related examples are clearly present and deployment-relevant. The examples do not contain real secrets, so the concern is safe usage rather than embedded leakage.

content.user_title: Deploy Google ADK Agents to Cloud
content.value_statement: Deploying ADK agents requires cloud target choices, IAM setup, secrets, and verification steps. This skill gives structured Google Cloud deployment guidance for Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, GKE, and CI/CD.
actual_capabilities: Compares Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE deployment targets for ADK agents.
Explains agents-cli deploy flags for projects, regions, service accounts, secrets, scaling, and dry runs.
Guides CI/CD setup with GitHub Actions or Google Cloud Build using Workload Identity Federation.
Documents Secret Manager, service account, IAM, and Cloud SQL permission patterns.
Provides testing paths for deployed agents with agents-cli, curl, Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE.
Covers rollback, troubleshooting, Terraform custom infrastructure, and production promotion workflows.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not validate the user cloud environment before commands run.
It assumes projects were created or enhanced with google-agents-cli scaffold conventions.
It focuses on Google Cloud deployment targets and does not support non-GitHub CI/CD providers directly.
Some referenced Google Cloud features, previews, and CLI flags may change over time.

use_cases.title: Choose a Deployment Target
use_cases.description: Compare Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE for an ADK agent before committing to an infrastructure path.

use_cases.title: Set Up Production CI/CD
use_cases.description: Configure staging and production deployment flow with Terraform, GitHub Actions, Cloud Build, and approval gates.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Deployment Failures
use_cases.description: Investigate IAM, Secret Manager, Cloud SQL, timeout, rollback, and endpoint testing issues after deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Target
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE for my ADK agent. Ask for missing requirements before recommending a target.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy to Development
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through a dev deployment for my scaffolded ADK agent. Include approval checkpoints, required flags, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Production CI/CD
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a production CI/CD setup for my ADK agent with staging and production projects. Include IAM, WIF, secrets, and approval gates.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Failed Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this ADK deployment failure. Identify likely causes across IAM, Secret Manager, Cloud SQL, Terraform state, logs, and runtime target settings.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy an ADK agent that needs event triggers and private database access.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to use Cloud Run or GKE, with required VPC, Cloud SQL, Secret Manager, service account, and Terraform considerations.

output_examples.input: Set up CI/CD for staging and production Google Cloud projects.
output_examples.output: A staged checklist covering repository setup, Workload Identity Federation, Terraform creation, secrets, test stages, and production approval.

output_examples.input: My deployed Cloud Run agent returns 403 when tested.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting path that checks identity tokens, IAP, service account permissions, ingress settings, and Cloud Logging evidence.

best_practices: Get explicit human approval before running deployment, infrastructure, or production promotion commands.
Store credentials in Secret Manager and pass secret references instead of raw secret values.
Use Terraform-managed infrastructure for production and verify changes with tests and logs after deployment.
anti_patterns: Running deploy or terraform apply commands without confirming the target project and environment.
Pasting real API keys, database passwords, or GitHub tokens into prompts, logs, or shell history.
Manually creating production cloud resources that should be tracked in Terraform state.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy an agent automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command patterns. A user or agent must still run commands with explicit approval.

faq.question: Which deployment targets does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Agent Runtime, Cloud Run, and GKE for Google ADK agents.

faq.question: Can it help with production CI/CD?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains GitHub Actions, Google Cloud Build, Workload Identity Federation, Terraform, staging, and production approval flows.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use Google Secret Manager and pass secret names or versions. Do not place raw secret values in prompts or logs.

faq.question: Does it support GitLab or Bitbucket directly?
faq.answer: No. The documented agents-cli CI/CD flow focuses on GitHub and uses provider-specific Terraform and workflow files.

faq.question: What are the main risks?
faq.answer: The main risks are cloud resource changes, IAM changes, network exposure, and credential mishandling if commands are used carelessly.
